display_name: Chicago, Rock Island and Pacific Railroad

segments.to: Des Moines, Iowa / Kansas City, Missouri
segments.distance: approximately 340 miles to Des Moines; 500 miles to Kansas City
segments.travel_time: 10-14 hours to Des Moines; 16-20 hours to Kansas City (1880s-1890s)
segments.cost: $8-$15 emigrant class from Chicago to Kansas City (1880s-1890s). The Rock Island offered special excursion fares for land-seekers heading to the Oklahoma Territory.
segments.conditions: The Rock Island was the first railroad to bridge the Mississippi River (1856, at Rock Island-Davenport) and ran due west across Iowa through some of the richest farmland in America. Emigrant cars carried families from Chicago's immigrant neighborhoods — Poles, Czechs, Swedes, Germans — who had arrived in America only weeks or months before boarding the westbound train. At Kansas City, the line connected with branches running south into Indian Territory and the Oklahoma District. In the weeks before each Oklahoma land run, the Rock Island ran special excursion trains packed with would-be settlers, their covered wagons loaded on flatcars behind them.

segments.from: Kansas City, Missouri (or Caldwell / Arkansas City, Kansas)
segments.to: El Reno / Oklahoma City / Chickasha, Oklahoma Territory
segments.cost: $5-$10 excursion fare to the Oklahoma Territory for the land runs (1889, 1891, 1893, 1901). Regular emigrant fare after settlement: $8-$15 from Kansas City.
segments.conditions: The Rock Island was THE railroad of the Oklahoma land runs. On April 22, 1889, at noon, the starting gun fired and thousands of settlers raced from the borders of the Unassigned Lands to stake claims — many of them had ridden Rock Island trains to the border towns of Caldwell and Arkansas City, Kansas. The railroad itself ran through the center of the Oklahoma District, and settlers who could not afford horses or wagons simply jumped off the slow-moving train at their chosen spot and ran to drive a stake. The 1893 Cherokee Outlet run brought over 100,000 people — the largest land run in history — with Rock Island trains delivering settlers to El Reno and other staging points along the line. Train platforms were jammed; men rode on the roofs of cars and clung to the sides.

segments.distance: approximately 300 miles to Amarillo; 450 miles to Tucumcari
segments.cost: $10-$18 from El Reno to Amarillo (1900s-1910s); the Rock Island's 'Golden State Limited' offered through service from Chicago to the Southwest.
segments.conditions: West of Oklahoma City, the Rock Island stretched across the red-earth cotton country of western Oklahoma and into the Texas Panhandle — flat, windy, and dry. The railroad opened this country to settlement in the early 1900s, bringing families who broke sod on 160-acre homestead claims in land that received barely enough rainfall for dryland farming. Dust storms were a fact of life long before the 1930s Dust Bowl made them famous. The tracks ran straight as a ruled line across country where you could see the water tower of the next town twenty miles ahead.

who_used_it: Oklahoma land-run settlers (1889, 1891, 1893, 1895, 1901) — the Rock Island delivered more settlers to more Oklahoma land openings than any other railroad. Midwestern farm families heading for cheap land in Oklahoma and the Texas Panhandle. Czech and Bohemian settlers who established communities in central Oklahoma (Prague, Yukon). African American settlers heading to the all-Black towns of Oklahoma Territory (Langston, Boley, Clearview) who rode the Rock Island and its connecting lines. Cotton farmers who shipped their harvest east on the same tracks they had arrived on. The railroad's popular culture legacy endures in Lead Belly's song 'Rock Island Line.'
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} rode the Rock Island Line from {origin} to the edge of {destination}, where the only thing standing between them and 160 acres of free land was a starting gun, a fast horse, and every other family on the train who wanted the same quarter-section.
source_references: William Edward Hayes, 'Iron Road to Empire: The History of 100 Years of the Progress and Achievements of the Rock Island Lines' (Simmons-Boardman, 1953)
Stan Hoig, 'The Oklahoma Land Rush of 1889' (Oklahoma Historical Society, 1984)
H. Craig Miner, 'The Corporation and the Indian: Tribal Sovereignty and Industrial Civilization in Indian Territory, 1865-1907' (University of Missouri Press, 1976)
Berlin B. Chapman, 'The Founding of El Reno' (Chronicles of Oklahoma, Vol. 34, 1956)
